overview.title: Overview & Start Here
overview.subtitle: New to Swamp Fox, new to insurance, or just looking for the map.

cards.title: What you'll find here
body: A glossary of 800+ insurance terms explained in plain English.
Reference cards grouped by topic — risk management, insurance operations, legal principles, auto, and life/health.
Role-focused playbooks for daily reference, account managers, loss control, and risk mitigation.
Direct links to federal and industry resources (FEMA, OSHA, FBI, USGS, NIST, CDC, EPA).

cards.title: How to use it
body: Type in the search bar to find any term instantly — works across glossary and reference cards.
Press / from anywhere to jump to search; press Esc to clear.
Use the sidebar to browse by topic.
Click the arrow next to an external resource to open it in a new tab.

cards.title: What insurance does
body: Insurance transfers the financial risk of an unexpected loss from one party (the insured) to another (the insurer) in exchange for a premium.
The insurer pools premiums from many insureds and uses that pool to pay claims for the few who actually suffer losses.
Works because losses are uncertain at the individual level but predictable across a large group.

cards.title: The core parts of any policy
body: Declarations — who is insured, what is insured, how much coverage, and for how long.
Insuring Agreement — what the insurer promises to do.
Exclusions — what the policy does NOT cover.
Conditions — what the insured has to do to keep coverage (pay premiums, report claims promptly, cooperate with investigation).
Definitions — what specific words mean in this policy.

cards.title: Premium, deductible, limit
body: Premium: the price the insured pays for the policy.
Deductible: the amount the insured pays out of pocket before the insurer starts paying on a claim.
Limit: the most the insurer will pay for a covered loss (per occurrence) or over the policy period (aggregate).
Higher deductibles lower premium but increase out-of-pocket cost at claim time.

cards.title: Personal lines vs. commercial lines
body: Personal lines cover individuals and families — homeowners, renters, auto, personal umbrella, life, health.
Commercial lines cover businesses — commercial property, general liability, workers comp, commercial auto, professional liability, cyber, D&O.
Different forms, different regulations, different underwriting approaches.
At Swamp Fox, most of our book is commercial (trucking, logging, timber, forestry) plus some personal lines.

cards.title: What an agency does
body: An agency represents insurance companies (carriers) and sells their products to insureds.
Producers (agents) find and service clients; account managers handle the day-to-day servicing.
The agency earns commission on premium and sometimes profit sharing on book performance.
Independent agents (like Swamp Fox) represent multiple carriers, so we can shop the market to find the best fit.

cards.title: The insurance cycle
body: Soft market — lots of capacity, carriers compete hard, underwriting is relaxed, rates drop.
Hard market — capacity shrinks (usually after big losses), carriers tighten underwriting, rates rise, some risks become hard to place.
Cycles typically last 3-10 years.
A hard market is the best time to demonstrate an agency's value through market access and relationships.

cards.title: Why underwriting matters
body: Carriers underwrite to decide whether to accept a risk and at what price.
Good submissions = complete applications + clean loss runs + narrative context + financial details.
A well-prepared submission gets better pricing and better terms than a rushed one — even for the same risk.
Submissions go through the 'eyes' of the underwriter; tell the story the way you'd want to hear it.

daily_reference.title: Daily Reference
daily_reference.subtitle: Quick answers for questions that come up during the day.

cards.title: "What's my deductible?"
body: Check the Declarations page — typically Page 1 of the policy.
Property and auto each have their own deductibles.
Wind/hail and hurricane deductibles may be separate and higher (often a percentage of dwelling value).
Deductibles apply PER occurrence, not per policy period.

cards.title: "Does my policy cover this?"
body: Never give a coverage opinion without reviewing the actual policy language.
Check declarations for limits and covered property/perils.
Check exclusions before confirming coverage.
When in doubt: report the claim — let the carrier make the coverage determination, not the agency.
Document every coverage conversation in the agency management system.

cards.title: "Can I add my kid to my auto policy?"
body: Yes, in most cases — but rates can change significantly, especially for male drivers under 25.
A teen with a clean record is cheaper to add than to get their own policy.
A teen with tickets or accidents may make the parent's policy unaffordable.
Consider named driver exclusion only as a last resort — any accident caused by the excluded driver results in no coverage.

cards.title: "I didn't get my renewal notice"
body: Check billing status — was there a non-pay cancellation?
Verify address on file is current.
Check the carrier's online portal for policy status.
If truly non-renewed: find out why (loss history, underwriting changes, market exit) and start remarketing immediately.
State law typically requires 30-60 days advance notice of non-renewal.

cards.title: "How much coverage do I need?"
body: For property: enough to rebuild at current construction costs — not market value.
For liability: enough to protect your assets. Minimum limits protect the state, not you.
Umbrella policies add inexpensive high-limit protection over auto and homeowners.
Commercial limits should consider worst-case scenario claim + defense costs.

cards.title: "Why did my premium go up?"
body: Rate changes filed with the state — affect all policyholders in a class.
Changes to your specific risk — new driver, new vehicle, claims, mod factor changes.
Inflation — replacement costs and medical costs rise; limits follow.
Territory changes — if you moved, rates may be different.
Always pull the full comparison before answering — carrier portals usually show the specific line items that changed.

cards.title: First response checklist
body: Safety first — make sure everyone involved is safe.
Document the scene — photos, names, contact info for witnesses and parties.
Report to carrier promptly — delay can void coverage.
Do not admit fault or sign anything from other parties' insurers.
Keep all receipts related to the loss — additional living expenses, repairs, replacements.
Get the claim number and assigned adjuster contact info.

cards.title: What to tell clients about adjusters
body: The carrier's staff adjuster works for the insurance company.
Independent adjusters are contracted by carriers during heavy claim volume.
Public adjusters represent the policyholder — at a percentage of the settlement.
The insured has the right to ask for an appraisal if they disagree on damage amounts.
Always keep the client-carrier-adjuster communication documented in the agency system.

cards.title: Subrogation in plain language
body: If someone else caused the loss, the carrier can pursue that party after paying the claim.
If the carrier recovers from the responsible party, the insured may get the deductible back.
Insureds should not settle with at-fault parties or sign releases without carrier input — it can destroy subrogation rights.

cards.title: Total loss basics
body: Vehicle is a total loss when repair + salvage exceeds actual cash value (threshold varies by state, 70-80% typical).
Carrier pays the actual cash value minus deductible.
Gap insurance fills the difference between ACV and loan balance.
Loaner/rental coverage expires at the settlement — not when the client buys a new car.

cards.title: ACORD 25 — what it is and isn't
body: It's evidence of insurance — confirms coverages are in place.
It is NOT an insurance contract; does not amend or extend coverage.
Adding "Certificate Holder" does not make them an insured — that requires an endorsement to the underlying policy.
Primary/non-contributory and waiver of subrogation require endorsement too, not just certificate wording.

cards.title: Additional insured vs. certificate holder
body: Certificate Holder: gets notified of changes or cancellation — no coverage rights.
Additional Insured: actual coverage under the policy for specified liability — requires carrier-issued endorsement.
Blanket AI endorsement may cover "any party required by contract" — check the specific form.
Most common contract requirement errors: certificate shows AI but endorsement was never issued.

cards.title: Common contract requirements
body: Additional insured status for the contract counterparty.
Primary and non-contributory basis.
Waiver of subrogation in favor of the counterparty.
Specific minimum limits (often $1M/$2M CGL, $1M auto, statutory workers comp).
AM Best rating minimum (usually A- VII or better).
30 days advance notice of cancellation — carriers often won't endorse this, so certificates say 'will endeavor to' instead.

account_manager.title: Account Manager Playbook
account_manager.subtitle: Workflows, checklists, and standards for commercial account service.

cards.title: 120 days out — start the renewal
body: Pull loss runs from incumbent carrier (most require 30-day notice).
Review changes in the client's operations — new locations, new equipment, acquisitions, changes in revenue.
Flag accounts with adverse loss experience for early market conversation.
Update exposure basis: payroll (WC), revenue (GL), vehicles (auto), building values (property).
Identify whether we'll market or simply renew with incumbent.

cards.title: 90 days out — prepare the submission
body: Complete ACORD 125 (base), 126 (GL), 140 (property), 130 (WC) as applicable.
Pull MVRs on all listed drivers.
Gather 5 years of loss runs.
Narrative: describe the operation, key controls in place, management response to any losses.
Financial statements for larger accounts — underwriters want to see the balance sheet.
Current policy and all endorsements for coverage comparison.

cards.title: 60-30 days out — market and quote
body: Submit to 3+ carriers unless single-market placement is already established.
Track submission status; follow up weekly.
Compare proposals on coverage (not just premium): limits, deductibles, endorsements, exclusions.
Prepare proposal for client with side-by-side comparison.
Flag any coverage reductions — don't let a lower price mask a broader exclusion.

cards.title: Bind and deliver
body: Signed application for the selected carrier.
Binder confirmation from the carrier.
Issue certificates to required parties before the expiration of old policy.
Ensure additional insured endorsements are issued on the new policy.
Schedule the policy delivery meeting; don't email-and-forget.
Document everything in the agency management system.

cards.title: Response time targets
body: New quote requests: acknowledge within 4 business hours.
Certificate requests: turnaround same business day if received before 3pm.
Endorsements: submit to carrier within 24 hours of client request.
Claims reporting: within the carrier's required window, typically 24 hours.
Coverage questions: never answer outside documented policy language. When in doubt, escalate.

cards.title: Documentation discipline
body: Every client interaction logged in the agency management system.
Every coverage conversation documented with date, participants, and conclusion.
Every client-requested change confirmed in writing (email acceptable) — verbal requests are an E&O risk.
Proposals in writing, signed acceptance for coverage reductions.
Follow-up reminders for commitments — to clients, carriers, and internal team.

cards.title: E&O risk points
body: Failing to procure coverage the client requested.
Failing to notify of non-renewal in time to replace coverage.
Recommending coverage without understanding the exposure.
Answering a coverage question incorrectly ("that's covered").
Allowing coverage to lapse due to billing or payment issues.
Not confirming additional insured endorsements were actually issued.
Document, document, document.

cards.title: Know your markets
body: Admitted carriers with rating above A- are typical go-to markets.
E&S (surplus lines) markets for risks the standard market declines — document declinations.
Specialty programs — trucking, logging, timber — with unique forms and endorsements.
Each carrier has an underwriting appetite — know yours before you submit.
Bad submissions hurt agency reputation — underwriters remember.

cards.title: Working with underwriters
body: Tell the story — a good narrative turns a marginal account into a quotable risk.
Highlight positive controls: safety programs, driver training, maintenance programs.
Address loss history head-on — don't hide it, explain what changed.
Respond to underwriter questions the same day where possible.
When declined, ask why — it teaches what that market wants to see next time.

loss_control.title: Loss Control Playbook
loss_control.subtitle: Practical loss control — before, during, and after the survey.

sections.heading: Preparing a client for a loss control visit

cards.title: Before the survey
body: Confirm who will be on-site — owner, safety manager, operations manager.
Gather written programs: OSHA-required plans, driver files, equipment maintenance records.
Walk the premises from the consultant's perspective — fix the easy items first.
Housekeeping, exit signs, fire extinguishers, blocked electrical panels — the low-hanging fruit.
Review prior recommendations and have status updates ready.

cards.title: During the survey
body: Be honest — misrepresentation comes back worse than disclosed problems.
Ask questions — the consultant's time is the cheapest loss control available.
Take notes on what they flag; ask for priorities.
Walk-through is a good time to ask about best practices from other similar operations.

cards.title: After the survey
body: Expect the report within 2-4 weeks.
Go through each recommendation with the client — prioritize mandatory items.
Track completion — carriers check on open recommendations.
Unaddressed recommendations can lead to premium surcharges, non-renewal, or denied claims tied to the recommendation.
Document completion with photos and dates.

sections.heading: Hierarchy of controls — how to think about any hazard

cards.title: 1. Elimination
body: Remove the hazard entirely.
Example: replace a hazardous chemical with a safer alternative or eliminate a process that creates the hazard.
Most effective — and usually the hardest to implement.

cards.title: 2. Substitution
body: Replace a hazard with something less dangerous.
Example: switch from solvent-based to water-based paint.
Still effective without redesigning the whole operation.

cards.title: 3. Engineering controls
body: Isolate people from the hazard through physical means.
Examples: machine guards, ventilation hoods, noise-reducing enclosures, automatic shut-offs.
Works regardless of worker behavior.

cards.title: 4. Administrative controls
body: Change how people work to reduce exposure.
Examples: work rotation, training, procedures, signage, supervision.
Requires ongoing compliance — easier to fail.

cards.title: 5. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)
body: Last line of defense — hard hats, safety glasses, hearing protection, gloves, respirators.
Only protects the wearer, and only if worn correctly.
Never the only control — always paired with controls higher on the hierarchy.

cards.title: Fleet / commercial auto
body: Written driver qualification standards — minimum age, MVR review.
Pre-trip and post-trip inspections documented.
Telematics / GPS for monitoring speed, hard braking, hours of service.
Post-accident drug/alcohol testing program.
Regular vehicle maintenance schedule with records.
Return-to-work program for injured drivers.

cards.title: Property / fire protection
body: Sprinkler systems inspected annually and 5-year internal inspection.
Fire extinguishers inspected monthly, serviced annually.
Hot work permit program — fire watch required 30+ minutes after work.
Housekeeping — combustibles stored at least 18 inches from sprinkler heads.
Smoke detectors and central station alarm monitoring.
Electrical panels cleared of storage, 36-inch clearance maintained.

cards.title: Workers compensation
body: Written safety program.
New-hire safety orientation documented.
Job hazard analyses for high-risk tasks.
Safety committee meeting monthly.
Incident investigation for every recordable — and every near-miss when possible.
Early return-to-work program — bring injured workers back to modified duty.
Post-accident drug testing where legally permissible.

cards.title: General liability
body: Slip-and-fall prevention — matting, signage, documented floor cleaning schedule.
Contractor insurance requirements — certificates and additional insured endorsements.
Written hold-harmless agreements in leases and service contracts.
Premises security — lighting, cameras, access control, alarm monitoring.
Product quality control / incident documentation for product liability exposures.

risk_mitigation.title: Risk Mitigation Strategies
risk_mitigation.subtitle: Practical prevention and mitigation for common business exposures.

cards.title: Four options for any risk
body: Avoid — don't do the thing that creates the risk (e.g., don't enter a market where you can't get insurance).
Reduce / Control — use loss prevention and loss control to lower frequency or severity.
Transfer — insurance, contractual risk transfer (hold-harmless, additional insured, waivers).
Retain — decide to absorb the risk (self-insure, higher deductible, self-insured retention).
Most real-world risk programs combine all four.

cards.title: Pre-loss vs. post-loss thinking
body: Pre-loss — training, inspections, maintenance, safety programs. Reduces frequency.
Post-loss — sprinklers, emergency response, business continuity. Reduces severity.
Severity controls are often physical (engineered systems); frequency controls are often behavioral (training, procedures).
A complete program addresses both — one without the other leaves gaps.

cards.title: Hurricane / high wind
body: Roof straps and hurricane clips on frame construction.
Impact-rated windows or shutters in coastal exposure.
Garage door reinforcement — a failed garage door pressurizes the building and pops the roof.
Tree management — remove weak/leaning trees within fall distance of structures.
Written hurricane response plan, including evacuation triggers and shutdown procedures.
FEMA Protect Your Property from High Winds series is the core reference.

cards.title: Flood
body: Confirm flood zone — FEMA's flood maps at hazards.fema.gov.
Elevate critical equipment (HVAC, electrical) above base flood elevation.
Flood insurance — mortgage lenders require it in SFHA zones; everyone in high-exposure areas should have it.
30-day waiting period for NFIP policies unless purchased at mortgage closing.
Document property with photos/videos before the event, not after.

cards.title: Wildfire
body: Defensible space — 30-100 feet of cleared, low-vegetation zone around structures.
Non-combustible roofing, siding, and decking in wildfire-prone areas.
Vents screened to prevent ember intrusion.
Evacuation plan, including pets and key documents.
Photo/video inventory of property and contents stored off-site.

cards.title: Earthquake
body: Seismic retrofit for older buildings in earthquake zones.
Secure heavy furniture, equipment, and inventory to prevent toppling.
Automatic gas shut-off valves.
Earthquake insurance — separate policy, significant deductible (often 10-15% of dwelling value).
Business continuity plan assumes utilities, suppliers, and logistics are disrupted for days to weeks.

cards.title: Workplace violence prevention
body: Written workplace violence prevention policy, communicated to all employees.
Threat assessment team to evaluate warning signs.
Access control — badge readers, visitor logs, locked entries.
Employee assistance program for stressors that can escalate.
Active shooter training using the Run-Hide-Fight model.
FBI's 'Workplace Violence: Issues in Response' is the foundational reference.

cards.title: Employee dishonesty / fraud
body: Separation of duties — the person who writes checks should not reconcile the bank statement.
Mandatory vacation — schemes often unravel when the perpetrator is out of the office.
Background checks on employees with financial or operational access.
Fidelity/crime insurance covers losses from employee theft.
Regular internal audits — even informal ones — are a strong deterrent.

cards.title: Cyber threats
body: Multi-factor authentication on all business accounts — email, banking, agency management system.
Regular backups, tested restorations, and offline copies to defeat ransomware.
Employee training on phishing — the #1 attack vector.
Written incident response plan with roles, notifications, and contacts.
Cyber liability insurance — standalone policy; most property and CGL policies exclude cyber.
NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0 is the practical baseline.

cards.title: Business Impact Analysis (BIA)
body: Identify critical business functions — what must work for the business to survive.
Estimate the financial impact of disruption for each, over time (4 hours, 24 hours, 1 week).
Map dependencies — which systems, suppliers, people, and locations each function requires.
The output sets recovery priorities.

cards.title: Recovery time and recovery point objectives
body: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) — how long can a critical function be down?
RPO (Recovery Point Objective) — how much data loss is acceptable?
Shorter RTOs and RPOs require more investment in redundancy and backups.
Align RTO/RPO with the BIA — don't spend on resilience that doesn't support a critical function.

cards.title: Testing the plan
body: Tabletop exercise — discussion-based walk-through of a scenario. Lowest cost, highest value/hour.
Functional exercise — actually test specific systems (failover, communication trees, IT restore).
Full-scale exercise — simulate a real event with actual resource deployment.
After-Action Report — document what worked, what didn't, and action items.
Untested plans are wish lists, not plans.

additional_resources.title: Additional Resources
additional_resources.subtitle: Authoritative external references — federal, industry, and loss control.

cards.title: FEMA — Ready.gov
body: The official U.S. preparedness portal.
Hazard information sheets for every common hazard (flood, hurricane, wildfire, active shooter, cyberattack, pandemic).
"Are You Ready?" comprehensive guide.
12 Ways to Prepare checklist.

cards.title: FEMA — Multi-Hazard Mapping Platform
body: Interactive map showing multiple hazards — flood, earthquake, wildfire, hurricane.
Search any address to see risk layers.
Useful for underwriting, site selection, and client risk conversations.

cards.title: FEMA — Building Science Publications
body: Protect Your Property from High Winds series.
FEMA 426: Mitigation against Terrorist Attacks Against Buildings.
FEMA 427: Design of Commercial Buildings to Mitigate Terrorist Attacks.
FEMA 430: Site and Urban Design for Security.
FEMA 459: Incremental Protection for Existing Commercial Buildings.

cards.title: USGS — Natural Hazards
body: Earthquake Hazards Program — fault maps, ground-shaking models.
Landslide Hazards Program — slope stability and susceptibility mapping.
Volcano Hazards Program — monitoring for active U.S. volcanoes.

cards.title: OSHA Compliance Assistance
body: Quick Start guides by industry.
Hazard identification tools and self-inspection checklists.
Small Business Handbook.

cards.title: OSHA — Workplace Heat Illness Prevention
body: Heat stress prevention — hydration, rest, shade, acclimatization.
Required guidance for outdoor and hot indoor work environments.
Heat-related illness emergency response protocols.

cards.title: NIOSH — Workplace Safety Research
body: National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health.
Occupational health research, PPE testing, and industry-specific hazards.
Extensive library of free downloadable publications.

cards.title: FBI — Workplace Violence: Issues in Response
body: FBI's guide for preventing and responding to workplace violence.
Threat assessment methodology.
Post-incident support and investigation protocols.

cards.title: FMCSA — Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
body: Regulatory home for interstate commercial motor carriers.
Safety regulations, guidance, and enforcement actions.
Pre-employment screening tools.
Hours of Service compliance resources.

cards.title: FMCSA — SAFER Safety Record Lookup
body: Public database of carrier safety records.
Operating authority, insurance status, and safety measures.
CSA scores and BASIC category rankings.
Used by shippers, brokers, and insurance underwriters.

cards.title: FMCSA — Drug & Alcohol Clearinghouse
body: Federal database of drug and alcohol violations by commercial drivers.
Required employer queries before hiring and annually for current drivers.
Driver self-query available.

cards.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0
body: Voluntary framework: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
Widely adopted across industries as a baseline.
Free implementation guides and profiles by industry.

cards.title: NIST Computer Security Resource Center
body: Central NIST cybersecurity publications portal.
SP 800-30: Risk Management Guide for IT Systems.
SP 800-100: Information Security Handbook for Managers.
SP 800-14: Generally Accepted Principles for Securing IT Systems.
SP 800-12: Introduction to Computer Security.

cards.title: CISA — Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency
body: Federal agency for cybersecurity and critical infrastructure.
Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog.
Free cyber hygiene services for critical infrastructure.
Ransomware guidance and incident response resources.

cards.title: EPA — Risk Assessment Portal
body: Environmental risk assessment methodology.
Human health and ecological risk guidance.
Chemical safety and pollution prevention resources.

cards.title: CDC — Filtration for Building Environments
body: Guidance for protecting buildings from airborne chemical, biological, or radiological threats.
HVAC design and filtration upgrade recommendations.
Relevant for pandemic preparedness and security planning.

cards.title: NIOSH Pocket Guide to Chemical Hazards
body: Reference source for chemical hazard recognition.
Exposure limits, health effects, PPE recommendations.
Searchable and free.

cards.title: Insurance Information Institute (III)
body: Industry data, statistics, and educational resources.
Facts + Statistics reports by line of business.
Consumer-facing explanations useful for client conversations.

cards.title: NAIC — National Association of Insurance Commissioners
body: Home of state insurance regulators.
Consumer insurance search tool (find an insurer's state-level data).
Model laws and regulatory guidance.

cards.title: AM Best
body: Insurance company financial strength ratings.
Industry reports and analysis.
Essential for underwriting and account placement.

cards.title: IIABA — Big 'I' Independent Agents
body: Trade association for independent insurance agents.
Advocacy, education, and agency resources.
E&O risk management and agency best practices.

cards.title: Hartford Steam Boiler (HSB)
body: Technical guidance on boiler, pressure vessel, and equipment safety.
Equipment start-up, shutdown, and maintenance fact sheets.
Core reference for equipment breakdown loss control.

cards.title: NFPA — National Fire Protection Association
body: Codes and standards for fire, electrical, and life safety.
NFPA 13 (sprinklers), NFPA 70 (electrical), NFPA 101 (life safety).
Core reference for property loss control.

cards.title: IBHS — Insurance Institute for Business & Home Safety
body: Independent, nonprofit research on property loss reduction.
FORTIFIED construction standard for wind-resistant building.
Loss-prevention research and retrofit guides.
